title: Custom Binary Wire Protocol
intention:
When this cluster is complete, a client library author can open a TCP connection to a Liminal node, negotiate a protocol version, authenticate, and then multiplex channels and conversations over that single connection with conversation lifecycle frames, typed message envelopes, backpressure signals, and causal context flowing natively in the wire format. The protocol should feel inevitable -- every frame carries exactly the information the bus needs to make routing, ordering, and pressure decisions, and nothing else. A developer reading a packet capture should be able to reconstruct the full conversation lifecycle without consulting application logs.

The protocol is the contract between the bus and every client SDK. Getting this right means SDKs in any language can be written against a stable, versioned binary spec rather than chasing an internal API. Getting it wrong means every SDK becomes a moving target and the bus's differentiators (conversations, typed messages, causal ordering, protocol-level backpressure) are lost in translation.
problem:
Liminal's core primitives -- conversations, typed messages, causal ordering, and backpressure signals -- have no existing wire protocol that can carry them. NATS's text protocol has no concept of conversations, no typed envelopes, and no backpressure signalling. gRPC adds per-message framing overhead unsuitable for high-throughput messaging and its request-response model fights the conversation lifecycle. Building on either would require bolting on the differentiators as application-level extensions, hiding them from the transport layer where they need to be visible for routing and pressure decisions.

Without a purpose-built protocol, client SDKs would need to implement conversation lifecycle tracking, schema validation, causal ordering, and backpressure interpretation in application code -- duplicating bus logic in every language. The bus itself would lose visibility into connection-level pressure because backpressure would be an application concern above the transport.
solution:
The protocol is a binary, length-delimited frame format designed for the conversation model. Every unit of communication on the wire is a frame. Frames are self-describing: a fixed-size header carries the frame type, flags, stream ID (for multiplexing), and payload length, followed by the variable-length payload.

Frame categories map to the bus's primitives. Lifecycle frames (Connect, ConnectAck, Disconnect) manage the connection. Conversation frames (ConversationOpen, ConversationMessage, ConversationClose, ConversationError) carry the conversation lifecycle that ADR-001 established as the fundamental unit. Subscription frames (Subscribe, Unsubscribe, SubscribeAck) bind a connection to channels with schema negotiation. Publish frames carry typed message envelopes into channels. Backpressure frames (Accept, Defer, Reject) carry the protocol-level signals from ADR-004. Ping/Pong frames handle keepalive.

Every message-carrying frame includes a causal context field: an optional parent reference or vector clock entry that the bus uses for per-causal-chain ordering (ADR-005). The bus reads causal context at the frame level, not the application level, so ordering decisions happen before dispatch.

Typed message envelopes wrap the application payload with a schema identifier (content type hash negotiated during subscription). The bus validates the envelope against the channel's declared type at publish time (ADR-002). Schema negotiation happens in the Subscribe/SubscribeAck exchange: the subscriber declares what schemas it accepts, the bus confirms which schema version will be used for that subscription.

Multiplexing is stream-based: each conversation or subscription gets a stream ID within the connection. Frames carry their stream ID in the header, allowing multiple concurrent conversations and subscriptions over a single TCP connection. Stream 0 is reserved for connection-level control (Connect, Ping, Disconnect). Stream allocation is client-initiated for publishes and subscriptions, server-initiated for incoming conversation offers.

Backpressure signals are per-stream. When a consumer's declared capacity (max-in-flight from the subscription) is reached, the bus sends Defer frames on that stream. When the consumer is overwhelmed beyond its buffer, the bus sends Reject frames. Accept frames confirm delivery. Producers receive these signals on their publish stream and can adapt their rate.

Protocol versioning is negotiated in the Connect/ConnectAck exchange. The client sends its supported version range, the server selects the highest mutually supported version. Version changes follow additive-only rules: new frame types can be added, existing frame layouts are never changed. Unknown frame types are skipped (length-delimited framing makes this safe).

The protocol module is structured as codec (encode/decode), frame definitions (types and serialization), and connection state machine (lifecycle transitions). The codec is synchronous and zero-copy where possible -- it operates on byte buffers, not async streams. The connection state machine enforces valid frame sequences (no Publish before Connect, no Subscribe before authentication).

Authentication is carried in the Connect frame as an opaque token. The bus validates the token and responds with ConnectAck (success) or ConnectError (failure with reason code). The protocol does not define authentication mechanisms -- it carries tokens and results. Mechanism selection (mTLS, JWT, API key) is a deployment concern above the protocol.

Error handling is explicit: every error condition has a frame (ConnectError, ConversationError, PublishError, SubscribeError) with a numeric reason code and optional human-readable message. The protocol never silently drops frames -- every frame that cannot be processed generates an error response on the same stream.

principles.text: Every bus primitive is visible at the wire level. Conversations, typed envelopes, backpressure, and causal context are frame-level constructs, not application-level encodings hidden in opaque payloads.
Frames are self-describing and length-delimited. Any frame can be skipped without understanding its payload. This is what makes protocol versioning safe.
No silent failures. Every frame the bus cannot process generates an explicit error frame on the same stream. Dropped connections are detected by keepalive, not by silence.
The codec is synchronous and zero-copy. Encoding and decoding operate on byte slices, not async streams. The async boundary is the transport layer above the codec.
Backpressure flows in-band. Pressure signals travel on the same stream as the messages they regulate, not on a separate control channel. A stream's pressure state is self-contained.
Authentication is transport, not mechanism. The protocol carries tokens and results. It does not define how tokens are created or validated.
Additive-only versioning. New frame types may be added. Existing frame layouts are never changed. Clients skip unknown frame types by reading their length prefix.

goals: A client can connect, authenticate, subscribe to a channel, publish a typed message, receive backpressure feedback, and disconnect using only the defined frame types.
Multiple conversations and subscriptions multiplex over a single TCP connection with independent stream-level backpressure.
The codec round-trips every frame type: encode then decode produces the original frame with byte-identical payloads.
Protocol version negotiation selects the highest mutually supported version and rejects incompatible connections with a clear error.
Causal context is present in every message-carrying frame and can be extracted at the frame level without deserializing the application payload.
The connection state machine rejects out-of-sequence frames (Publish before Connect, Subscribe before authentication) with appropriate error codes.

non_goals.reason: TLS wraps the TCP connection below the protocol layer. The protocol operates on cleartext byte streams; encryption is a transport concern.
The protocol carries opaque auth tokens. JWT validation, mTLS handshake, or API key lookup are bus-level concerns above the protocol (P6).
The protocol carries typed envelopes with a schema identifier and opaque payload bytes. How the payload is serialized (protobuf, JSON, BEAM external term format) is an SDK concern.

non_goals.text: Flow control at the TCP level
non_goals.reason: TCP flow control (window sizing, Nagle) is handled by the OS. The protocol's backpressure signals are application-level, complementing but not replacing TCP flow control.
Node-to-node communication uses beamr's distribution protocol. This cluster covers client-to-bus communication only.
A gateway translating HTTP/WS to the binary protocol is a separate cluster. The protocol is binary-native.

structure.note: Protocol module root: pub mod and pub use only (CN3)
Frame type enum, frame header, frame body variants
Encode/decode for frames: header parsing, length-delimited read/write, zero-copy where possible
ProtocolError type, reason codes, error frame construction
Typed message envelope: schema ID, causal context, payload bytes
Causal context representation: parent reference and vector clock entry
Connect/ConnectAck exchange: version negotiation, auth token, capability flags
Connection state machine: transition rules, valid frame sequences per state
Protocol version constants, version range, compatibility check
Stream ID allocation, stream table, per-stream state tracking
Accept/Defer/Reject frame handling, per-stream pressure state
Schema negotiation in Subscribe/SubscribeAck: accepted schemas, selected version

constraints.text: No file over 500 lines. If a module approaches this limit, extract into submodules.
No panics in production code. .unwrap() and .expect() only in tests.
mod.rs contains ONLY pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports. No logic.
All public types implement Debug. All error types implement std::error::Error via thiserror.
Frame header is fixed-size (type + flags + stream ID + payload length) so that any frame can be skipped without parsing its payload.
Codec encode/decode is infallible for valid frames: a successfully constructed frame always encodes, and valid wire bytes always decode. Malformed wire bytes produce ProtocolError, never a panic.
Stream 0 is reserved for connection-level control frames. Application streams use IDs >= 1.
Backpressure signals (Accept/Defer/Reject) travel on the same stream as the messages they regulate (P5).
Unknown frame types are skipped by reading their length prefix and discarding the payload, not by disconnecting (P7).
Every error condition produces an explicit error frame with a numeric reason code. No silent drops (P3).
